lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Well have we done, thrice valiant countrymen:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But all’s not done; yet keep the French the field.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The Duke of York commends him to your majesty.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Lives he, good uncle? thrice within this hour

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I saw him down; thrice up again, and fighting;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: From helmet to the spur all blood he was.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: In which array, brave soldier, doth he lie,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Larding the plain; and by his bloody side,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Yoke-fellow to his honour-owing wounds,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The noble Earl of Suffolk also lies.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Suffolk first died: and York, all haggled over,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Comes to him, where in gore he lay insteep’d,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And takes him by the beard; kisses the gashes

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: That bloodily did yawn upon his face;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He cries aloud “Tarry, my cousin Suffolk!

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: My soul shall thine keep company to heaven;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Tarry, sweet soul, for mine, then fly abreast,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: As in this glorious and well-foughten field

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: We kept together in our chivalry!”

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Upon these words I came and cheer’d him up:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He smiled me in the face, raught me his hand,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And, with a feeble gripe, says, “Dear my lord,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Commend my service to my sovereign.”

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: So did he turn and over Suffolk’s neck

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He threw his wounded arm and kiss’d his lips;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And so espoused to death, with blood he seal’d

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: A testament of noble-ending love.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The pretty and sweet manner of it forced

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Those waters from me which I would have stopp’d;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: But I had not so much of man in me,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And all my mother came into mine eyes

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And gave me up to tears.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I blame you not;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For, hearing this, I must perforce compound

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With mistful eyes, or they will issue too. Alarm.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But hark! what new alarum is this same?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The French have reinforced their scatter’d men:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then every soldier kill his prisoners;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Give the word through.

stage_directions.text: Alarums. Enter King Henry, and forces, Exeter, and others.

glosses.definition: the French still hold part of the battlefield — the battle isn't over
Edward, 2nd Duke of York — historically killed at Agincourt, possibly suffocated in his armour; the senior English casualty
rose three times after being knocked down

glosses.phrase: From helmet to the spur all blood
glosses.definition: covered in blood head to foot
enriching the plain with his flesh — 'larding' = basting with fat
paired with York by wounds deservingly won
Michael de la Pole, 3rd Earl of Suffolk — also killed at Agincourt
hacked to pieces
drenched in his own blood

glosses.phrase: My soul shall thine keep company to heaven
glosses.definition: York's vow to die alongside Suffolk — a deathbed companionship
knightly fellowship
reached (archaic)
weak grasp

glosses.phrase: over Suffolk’s neck / He threw his wounded arm
glosses.definition: homoerotic tableau of the two earls dying embraced — a heightened chivalric intimacy
married to death — the two earls wedded to each other in dying
a will (testament) sealed in blood — affirming their comradely love

glosses.phrase: all my mother came into mine eyes
glosses.definition: my feminine (motherly) emotional side welled up — Exeter confesses tears
negotiate with misty eyes — Henry has to deal with his tears too
a fresh alarm — trumpet signal of a new French push
the French have regrouped their scattered forces
Henry's order to kill the French prisoners — historically attested; controversial even in its own time. The scene presents it flatly, without commentary; the text leaves the ethics for the reader
pass the order along

annotations.title: The York-Suffolk death — chivalric tableau
annotations.body: Exeter's thirty-line report of the deaths of the Duke of York and the Earl of Suffolk is the play's most elaborate chivalric set-piece. The two earls die side by side, Suffolk first, then York 'all haggled over' comes to him; York takes Suffolk's beard, kisses the wounds on his face, and asks his soul to wait so they can fly to heaven together. The image of the two men 'espoused to death' with 'a testament of noble-ending love' is unmistakably homoerotic in its language and its staging — a male-male death-marriage. This is period chivalric idiom; it is also a tonal counterpoint to the harder action that is about to happen. Exeter's admission that he wept ('all my mother came into mine eyes / And gave me up to tears') is unusual for a commander; Henry's answer ('I blame you not; / For, hearing this, I must perforce compound / With mistful eyes') equally unusual for a king. The scene is building, in chivalric tears, an emotional charge that the next line — the order to kill the prisoners — will suddenly violate.
sources: Bruce Smith, Homosexual Desire in Shakespeare's England (1991)

annotations.title: 'Then every soldier kill his prisoners' — the play's moral crux
annotations.body: Henry's order to kill the French prisoners is the play's single most controversial moment, and Shakespeare does not soften it. 'But hark! what new alarum is this same? / The French have reinforced their scatter'd men: / Then every soldier kill his prisoners; / Give the word through.' Four lines. No debate, no hesitation, no later regret. Historically the order is attested: contemporary chroniclers (the Gesta Henrici Quinti and others) record that Henry gave this command when the baggage camp was attacked during the battle, fearing that the prisoners (who still had their weapons) would rise up and join their countrymen. The order was controversial even in its own time; some English knights refused to carry it out. The killing that followed was of noble prisoners who were, by the conventions of chivalric warfare, legally protected until ransomed. Shakespeare stages it flatly. The next scene (4.7) will let Gower interpret the order as retaliation for the French killing the camp-boys — but the play's chronology is deliberately blurred; in the text, Henry gives the order before he has heard about the boys. The moral complexity is exactly this: is Henry responding to a tactical emergency (the reinforcement alarm he mentions), a strategic calculation about armed prisoners, or a breach of the laws of war? The play refuses to adjudicate. Readers should hold the flatness of the stage-moment against the weight of what has been ordered.
sources: Gesta Henrici Quinti, ed. Frank Taylor & John S. Roskell (1975)
Anne Curry, Agincourt: A New History (2005)
Juliet Barker, Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle (2005)

trials.prompt: How does Exeter describe the death of York and Suffolk?
options: A chivalric death-marriage: the two earls 'espoused to death' with York kissing Suffolk's wounds; Exeter admits he wept; Henry admits he must 'compound / With mistful eyes' — unusual emotional frankness before the next line's order

trials.prompt: How does the play stage Henry's order to kill the French prisoners?
options: With a long soliloquy justifying it

In four lines — 'hark! what new alarum is this same? / The French have reinforced their scatter'd men: / Then every soldier kill his prisoners; / Give the word through.' No debate, no regret; the flatness itself is the play's statement
As a deferred decision never actually carried out

Yes — contemporary chroniclers (Gesta Henrici Quinti and others) record that Henry gave this command during the battle; it was controversial in its own time, with some English knights refusing to carry it out
Only in French sources hostile to Henry

trials.prompt: Why does the play deliberately blur the timing between Henry's order and the French killing the camp-boys?
options: To leave the moral question open: 4.6 has Henry give the order in response to a 'new alarum' of reinforcement, before any mention of the boys; 4.7 has Gower retroactively interpret the order as retaliation for the killed boys. The two framings are both on the page, neither adjudicated
The Folio printers made an error
Because Shakespeare wrote the scenes years apart
